titleInfo.title: The Buddhist "Monastery" and the Indian Garden: Aesthetics, Assimilations, and the Siting of Monastic Establishments

mods.abstract: Early Buddhist monasteries resembles Indian gardens in several aspects: Even the name vihāra or ārāma originally refers to gardens close to the city, a constructed and cultivated space, associated with erotic love. "Monks who redacted the Mūlasarvāstivāda-vinaya were fully familiar with the aesthetic values and erotic overtones associated with the garden in spring." Expressions for describing a Buddhist vihāra are the same as those found in Classical Sanskrit poetry. "Both garden and monastery were—according to our vinaya—the focus of sightseeing excursions on the part of well-to-do ladies..." Gardens were furnished with decorations, first of such are paintings. See description of the fifth-century garden at Sigiriya, Sri Lanka, by D. Ali, "Gardens in Early Indian Court Life," Studies in Histories, n.s. 19.2 (2003):221-52. Like gardens, Buddhist monasteries selected their siting according to the view from it, and were provided with (public) benches to enjoy that view. Schopen also names Western Cave sites in India (Satdhāra, Nal, Kanheri) with stone benches.

titleInfo.title: Journal of the American Oriental Society
